speaker: interviewer
text: 25th, 1996, in Camarillo, California. Mrs. Biniaz, you were telling us about your constant fear at Plaszow, and your experiences with Amon Goeth, the commandant. Did you see him often there?

speaker: interviewee
text: Quite often. You know, he took great pleasure frightening people, so he would enjoy walking through the camp with his two huge Great Danes, and he-- he got enormous pleasure out of putting fear into people. There was just-- he was a very evil man.

speaker: interviewer
text: I also distinctly remember one day, he came to Madritsch, spoke to my father, and all of a sudden we saw my father walking with him down the road. Well, it was incredible fear, because we didn't know that my father was going to return, you know? But he did-- he did come back. I don't know what it was all about.

speaker: interviewee
text: But you see, Goeth was-- Madritsch was friendly with Goeth, just as Schindler was friendly with Goeth, in a sense that they were supplying Goeth with all kinds of goodies, you know. That's how, you know, the bribery worked. And so we had a certain amount of protection, as long as we worked for Madritsch. Madritsch was a wonderful person-- he and his companion, Mr. Titsch. They were really classy-- two people who somehow just got caught. You know, there were a lot of Germans and Austrians who were caught in the situation, not of their doing, really, and they did the best they could. They did bring in food for us. Madritsch brought in loads of-- loaves of bread for the people-- for his workers-- and medications. They were very decent people.

speaker: interviewer
text: So how long did you actually work at the Madritsch factory?

speaker: interviewee
text: I worked at the Madritsch factory till the concentration camp closed-- which was, I believe-- now I have a hard time remembering whether it was September that the camp actually closed, and we were all shipped to Auschwitz.

speaker: interviewer
text: Well, It was at that time that Schindler came to Madritsch and made him a proposition, and said, you know, I'm taking my people to Czechoslovakia. Why don't you reestablish a factory in Czechoslovakia? And Madritsch said he didn't want to-- he had had enough of the whole thing.

speaker: interviewee
text: But Schindler said to him, well then, I'm making a list of people that I'm taking with me. And I am-- because we're friends, I want you to give me some names from your people. And that's how we got on the list-- on Schindler's list, and that's how we survived. It was really thanks to Madritsch, who was grateful, really, to my parents for having worked so well for him, you know, that he put us on the list. He put on quite a few people from the factory on the list. And that's how we got on.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you remember Madritsch's first name?

speaker: interviewee
text: I'm trying to think of his first name. I know that Titsch's first name was Raymond. Madritsch's first name? I may think of it, I don't know.

speaker: interviewer
text: So when they closed the concentration camp, how did you find out you were going to Auschwitz? Or did you find out you were going to Auschwitz?

speaker: interviewee
text: We found out when we ended up in Auschwitz. We were put on a train-- we thought we were-- the men were shipped earlier, and we-- the women were then shipped in the cattle cars. And it took quite a few days-- it took some time. We didn't really realize that we were getting-- going to Auschwitz till we arrived there in the middle of the night, and they emptied out the cars, and we saw where we were. And it was a very frightening experience.

speaker: interviewer
text: While you were in the cars those three days-- the cattle cars-- what was that like for you?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, it was hard. You know, it was-- we were all cramped together. We had no water, no food, nothing. But I think-- and the fear of not knowing where we were going-- you know, really, I mean, we didn't-- we were being-- the reason it took so long-- I mean, Krakow and Auschwitz are very close. It's only like 35 miles, or no more than 50 miles away. But because the Russians were approaching, there were many trains being shunted over to different sidelines-- styled rails, you know. That's why it took-- it didn't take three days. It just took a day and a half, I think. It took us three and a half days to go from Auschwitz to Czechoslovakia. That was the long trip. But we ended up in Auschwitz in the middle of the night, and that was a horrible experience.

speaker: interviewer
text: Describe what you remember.

speaker: interviewee
text: Fear, smell, burning flesh, shouting, orders, not knowing which way to-- music being played. Slush, on-- you know-- on-- the mud on the-- on the road. You know, the Germans barking at us-- get out, get out, get out, you know, from the cattle cars.

speaker: interviewer
text: Finding ourselves on this-- and realizing where we were. And you know, the fear that that was the end, you know? Were you with your mother at this time?

speaker: interviewee
text: All the time. I was with my mother all the time. And of course, the first place they took us to, they walked us into the sauna, which is the-- supposedly the delousing room, or whatever. But we, at that point, already had heard what happened in Auschwitz, so when they told us to strip and take our clothes off, and they shaved our heads and all that, and they shoved us into the-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] the shower room, you know, we didn't know what to expect, you know? Whether we were going to have the gas, or whether we were going to have actual water.

speaker: interviewer
text: It was a horrible experience, you know? When the water finally came, it was just like-- we couldn't believe it, you know. And what happened after that?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, they gave us clothes. I mean, they took our clothes away and they gave us their clothes, so we no longer had anything of our own anymore, at all, you know. And then they took us to the barracks.

speaker: interviewer
text: What were the barracks like?

speaker: interviewee
text: Cold. Very cold. I mean, Poland gets very cold starting from October on, you know? It was very cold. And the thing that-- you know, and they kept pulling us out of the barracks every-- and make us stand in front, and count us and count us, again in fives. So we would switch places, you know, so the person in front and the person in the back wouldn't get so cold, you know. We would take turns. And of course, food was very limited. So we were hungry, very hungry.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was the food like?

speaker: interviewee
text: Just a pot of watery soup and a little piece of bread. That was it. And the most important thing was to keep yourself clean, because the slightest pimple on your body, or anything, would right away make you go to the wrong side. Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: What did you do there during the day?

speaker: interviewee
text: Nothing. Literally nothing. There was nothing to do, except stand on-- you know, I mean, they kept us for hours outside, counting us. But there was-- you know, there was no work. We were waiting. We were in transition, supposedly. At least we found out that we were in transition. We couldn't figure out how we got there. We were supposed to have gotten-- you know, we were signed on a list, we were supposed to go to Brünnlitz to work.

speaker: interviewer
text: So we were just waiting. We didn't know what was happening. That was another thing, you know, not knowing, one way or the other. Are there any specific incidences that you remember from Auschwitz that you'd like to share with us?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, one specific incident is that I owe my life to Dr. Mengele's change of heart, you know. There was one day that they had took us through a selection again, and it was the only time that my mother and I were separated. She had gone to-- on the ruse of peeling potatoes or something, which she thought she could bring some potato skins back to-- to eat. Turned out, it wasn't that. I think they were supposed to give blood or something like that. But anyway, they took some of the women-- 35 or 40 women-- to do work or something. But the rest of the barrack was taken to-- for a selection. Again, we were told to strip and walk through. And on the first run through, Dr. Mengele pushed me to the left side with some of the older women. And then, I don't know what happened. He had a change of heart, and told everybody to go through again. And when we went through again, I just-- I don't know how I got the nerve, but I looked up at him and I said three words in German-- [SPEAKING GERMAN] and he let me go. He let me go to the right. And I ran like crazy, clutching my clothes in my hands, in the nude, out.

speaker: interviewer
text: How old were you at that time?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, I must have been 12. 12 and a half, almost 13. Real age-- according to the upped age, I was probably, what, two years older? So I would have been almost 14.

speaker: interviewer
text: What do you remember about Dr. Mengele that stands out in your mind?

speaker: interviewee
text: He was a devil. That's what I-- a handsome-- he was not a bad-looking guy, you know? But what I remember-- he had a pencil in his hand, and that's all he did was point with his pencil. That was Dr. Mengele.

speaker: interviewer
text: That was my one brief encounter, but I came out of it OK, you know? So I was lucky. So when your mother returned--

speaker: interviewee
text: My mother returned and found out that the barrack had been taken for selection, and she was frantic-- absolutely frantic. I remember having run out from the selection, and my mother returning and running up and down looking for me. I mean, she was absolutely frantic, you know? I can imagine, you know, what it would be like.

speaker: interviewer
text: So how long were you at Auschwitz, altogether?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, I'm trying to remember when he got us, finally. Schindler actually came-- and now, see, that's another thing-- I really didn't know that, that he actually came for us, till I found out later that he-- it was only through his interference that we finally got out, you know.

speaker: interviewer
text: Because all of us-- we were lined up to go. They lined us up again, and told us they were going to tattoo us-- tattoo our number on us. And I was even asking my mother, is it going to hurt? Is it going to hurt?

speaker: interviewee
text: And then, all of a sudden, they shoved us into the cars, and we went off without the tattoos. We couldn't-- you know, everything was just-- everything happened, and you didn't understand why. We were there, definitely, through a lot of snow and cold. So I would have to say we were there through November and part of December. I would think so, you know. And then it took us about three and a half days to make the trip from Auschwitz to-- my mother got very sick. She had pneumonia. She really got very, very sick. When we got to-- to Brünnlitz, you know, they came to-- Schindler came to the-- to the station to pick us up-- you know, to the railroad station-- we couldn't believe that we actually made it.

speaker: interviewer
text: What do you remember--

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, we saw the men behind the wire, you know? And we couldn't believe-- and they couldn't recognize us, because we were all shaved. They left us with hair, and got all their women without any hair, you know?

speaker: interviewer
text: So, the barracks-- I remember that we slept on top of the factory, and it was warm. I remember that. We felt good and warm.

speaker: interviewee
text: But he hadn't had the time, yet, to build any bunks, so we all slept on the floor. It was only later that he-- that they built bunks for us. And in this particular place, we shared two to a bunk-- so that was kind of nice to not to have to share with five people. They were triple bunks, you know-- three tiers. But the distinct memory is that it was warm. Again, he got us some soup and some coffee, and we each got a round little bowl and a pot-- which my mother still has to this day-- red enamelled pot, enamelled pan. The pan was for soup, the pot was for coffee-- which, you could call it coffee. I mean, it was just water, really-- and a piece of bread. But one of the problems-- you know, we felt safe with Schindler, but one of the problems was that there was really no food. At that point, this was towards the end of the war, and supplies were very hard to come by.

speaker: interviewer
text: People-- there were quite a few sick people at that point. The trip was very hard. In the cold cattle cars, a lot of people got sick. It was a problem, you know? What happened with your mother's pneumonia? My mother, she was absolutely remarkable. She was very, very sick, and there were no medications, you know. She got finished with the pneumonia, but she had kidney problems after that. She was very swollen towards the end of the-- ot the camp, you know.

speaker: interviewee
text: We all were kind of sick. In Plaszow, I had had my experiences. I had-- I forgot to say that-- I had contracted scarlet fever, and ended up at the epidemical hospital, where-- which was kind of dangerous at the time, you know, to be sick at all. But at the hospital, unfortunately, as I got over the scarlet fever, I caught typhoid fever, because there were no sanitary conditions. And I got over the typhoid fever, but that left me with jaundice. I was kind of yellow for a long time. And that left me with a liver impairment, which then flared up in Brünnlitz. And I ended up in the infirmary with kind of an enlarged liver. And it was only through the good graces of Mrs. Schindler-- Emily-- who every day came with a pot of farina that she had cooked herself, you know, and fed us-- fed us sick people, that we survived, really. Because that was the only nourishment that we got.

speaker: interviewer
text: So that was the story.

speaker: interviewee
text: I worked there, too. I mean, we all worked on the-- on the machinery.

speaker: interviewer
text: What did you do on the machinery?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, to start with, another girl and I-- we were the youngest there. According to Schindler, we had the small hands that were able to fit into the large machinery and clean the large machinery from inside out. And that's what we started doing. We cleaned the large machinery, you know. And then I worked on a lathe, and then I worked on a calibrating machine for a while. An interesting thing is, there was a row of us on the calibrating machine, you know, sitting there and measuring the items as they came off the assembly line, to make sure that they were correct. And they weren't, of course, but that was the idea. I mean, Schindler didn't want us to produce anything that was-- that would work.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did he tell you that?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, he did tell us that. I mean, he told the supervisors that, you know. And the calibrating machines were fixed in such a way that if somebody checked it, it would show right. But it wasn't right. But the interesting thing is, as we sat on the line, the-- you know, with the calibrating-- everybody talked about food. And they talked about the elegant dinners and elegant desserts that they would prepare and cook. I mean, it was incredible, the way they talked about, you know, fancy meals. As if that gave-- you konw, that's all they talked about was food. Because we were so hungry. I think that was the reason for it.

speaker: interviewer
text: While you were working at Brünnlitz, did you see a lot of Schindler there?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, he used to come on the factory floor quite often. As a matter of fact, one day he was visited by Mr. Titsch from Madritsch factory, who came to visit. And they came by, as I was cleaning one of the machines, and they stopped by, and Mr. Titsch spoke to me and asked me, you know, how things were, and if there was anything that I would want. And at the time, I was wearing still the clogs-- the wooden clogs-- and it was winter. And I said to Mr. Titsch, ah, if only we could have shoes. About a week and a half later, a load of-- well, now, they weren't shoes, but they had-- you know, they were wooden soles, but at least they had some sort of a canvas tops-- was delivered for the women. Now, I don't know whether Mr. Titsch did it or whether Schindler did it, but Schindler was with him, you know. It was nice to have gotten some shoes. And then another time, Goeth came to visit.

speaker: interviewer
text: What do you remember about that?

speaker: interviewee
text: I remember we were lined up to greet him, and Schindler walked through with Goeth. And of course, in Plaszow, you didn't dare lift your eyelids to look at Goeth. I mean, your head was always down. Well, we felt so protected by Schindler that we looked at him. For the first time, really looked in the guy's face. And it was just-- it was a good feeling, you know, to be able to look the monster in his face. It was even better later on in Poland, when we returned to Poland after the war, to find out that he had been caught, judged guilty, and was facing hanging. Just what he meted out to other people.

speaker: interviewer
text: So what do you remember about the end of your stay at Brünnlitz? How did things change? Were your SS guards still around? What happened?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, the SS-- it was interesting, because the war-- we heard-- we had underground radio that we listened to, so we knew that the war was over on the 7th of May. But-- and of course, Schindler left under the-- with some of the people who escorted him out of-- into the American zone. However, we were waiting for the Russians to liberate us. We were surrounded by the black shirts. There were huge groups of them still in the surrounding woods of Czechoslovakia-- or at that area, anyway. The SS-- from what we understand-- from what I remember, what I understood-- that the two grave-- even though we were under the protection of Schindler, there was a commandant that was in charge of the camp, of the labor camp. I mean, Schindler only used the labor force in his factory. The commandant was supposedly in charge of the prisoners. Supposedly, there were two huge graves dug already for us. Now, I don't know whether that is true or not, that's what I heard. Schindler-- we also heard that Schindler got the guards drunk in some way, and sent them off on a train, away. One guard-- and he gave the Jewish policemen guns to protect us while he went away. And so, for two days, we had protection from Jewish prisoners holding rifles against any marauders that might be on the outside. We understood there was one terrible guy, and somehow they caught him in some way and kept him. Because I know that, when the Russians liberated us, they hanged him, and they wanted us all to watch the hanging.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was this a German that was hung?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: Who did the hanging?

speaker: interviewee
text: The Russians. The Russian army was a very interesting army when they came in, unlike an army that you think marches to a-- to a goose-stepping beat, you know. They were very incredible. There were women, and young boys, and older men. It was just-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] they weren't in ranks of any sort, they just came, you know, with one of them on a horse to start with, to tell us that we were free and to tell us to leave the camp. Well, we were afraid. We did not know how to walk outside of the camp without walking in fives, you know. But after a while, you know, in a few days, we started back to Poland walking. And, you know, all the bridges were blown up, and all the roads were-- it took us-- let's see now, we were liberated on the 9th, and it took us, I would say, a good two weeks to get back to Krakow.

speaker: interviewer
text: This was the 9th of May, 1945?

speaker: interviewee
text: '45, Mm-hm.

speaker: interviewer
text: Before you get back to Krakow, let me just ask you-- did you see Schindler leave the camp?

speaker: interviewee
text: No I did not, no. I understand he left at night. There were some people who did accompany him, and some people who did see him leave, but I was not one of them. No.

speaker: interviewer
text: So after leaving the camp, how did you get back to Krakow?

speaker: interviewee
text: We walked, primarily. We walked, or we hitched some rides or with-- on carts, if people would take us. The Czechs were incredibly kind people, the kindest people I've met, really. They were wonderful. They really extended themselves. They shared whatever they had with us. Really good people.

speaker: interviewer
text: So, you know, by walking and by hiking and by hitchhiking, and doing all of those things, we finally got back to Krakow. Who were you with at the time?

speaker: interviewee
text: My mother and my father, the three of us, because we met in the last camp.

speaker: interviewer
text: And how old were you at this time?

speaker: interviewee
text: I was 13, almost 14, because my birthday was on the 28th of May.

speaker: interviewer
text: So what do you remember about going back to Krakow?

speaker: interviewee
text: I remember-- I remember that we weren't welcomed. Here we thought we had survived the war, and we would be, you know, welcomed, but we weren't at all. If there was any-- you know, that's when I really felt anti-Semitism. We were really told, you know, we shouldn't have survived, Hitler didn't do enough. You felt like you didn't want to stay there for a very long time. On the 28th of May, it was my birthday, and the only thing I wanted was to go to a movie. So my mother arranged for me to go to the movie-- but I didn't have any clothes. We were staying with some people, and I borrowed a skirt and a top from this lady. But the skirt-- I had to wind like three times around me. I had weighed 65 pounds, and I was as tall as I am now. So I was pretty skinny. Yeah, 35 kilos-- 70 pounds, yeah. But I had-- you know, I went to the movies. I remember to this day what I saw, it was Deanna Durbin. And it was some sort of a show called, in Polish, [SPEAKING POLISH]. I mean, it was some sort of a school for girls-- you know, adolescent girls. And I was-- to me it was absolutely charming, and she had a beautiful voice, and she was singing in it. But shock of shocks, I saw myself for the first time in a full-length mirror. I couldn't believe what I saw, you know. I mean, my hair had just started growing out, and I was skinny as anything, and I looked awful. You know, like a skeleton, practically. So that's how I remember my birthday. Anyway, I also realized that I didn't know anything. I had-- I didn't know how to write. I knew how to read, because one of the things I forgot to tell you was that in Plaszow, they brought books from all over to burn them. And the women who were in charge of burning the books used to steal them-- under penalty of death-- hide them on them, bring them back to the barracks, and pass them around to read at night, you know. And we would just-- so we read, you know, by the night light. I knew how to read, but I didn't know how to write. And I had lost-- you know, I was 14, and I only had two years of school. So the important thing was to get caught up.

speaker: interviewer
text: So I started learning as quickly as possible. My parents got me a tutor, and I tried to cram in as much as possible. Was this in Krakow?

speaker: interviewee
text: That was in Krakow. We got there, as I said, in May. And I had the summer to do it in, because I was trying to make it into school in September. And I took the exams, and I made it into the gymnasium. I made up six years of school in one summer, and made it into-- there was only two Jewish girls were accepted. I was one of them. However, in September, there was another pogrom in Poland. And it was at that point that my parents decided-- we-- well, they went back, really, to see if there was anybody left from the family. And since there was nobody left from the family that we knew of, they let my uncle know, in Israel, and also my uncle in the United States, that we survived. And my parents decided Poland was not the place to stay. And I was in school for about four weeks, just September. And in October, we smuggled over the border, out of Poland. And already at that point, Poland was closing its borders. And we smuggled in a Russian truck into Bratislava. And from Bratislava, we made our way into Prague-- back to Czechoslovakia. And from Prague, we went-- we waited there for about a week and a half, and then we were taken over the border-- the German-Czech border. In the middle of the night, we walked across. We missed the-- we made it through the Czech, but didn't quite make it behind the border of the Americans, but they let us in. And we made it into Hof, and from Hof into Landsberg, the displaced persons camp, where we stayed for a little while. And then into Mindelheim, where we stayed for a year and a half, and then the United States.

speaker: interviewer
text: Let me stop you right here. We're going to change the tape and we'll talk a little bit more about this.

speaker: interviewee
text: OK.
